behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure involves two modules — the HMAC verification middleware and the queue forwarding path. A Unit test of the HMAC function alone cannot verify the 'not forwarded' guarantee, because that guarantee depends on the handler correctly branching after the check and not calling the queue. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed — an in-process HTTP test framework (supertest) with a spy/mock queue is sufficient to verify both the 401 response and the absence of a queue write. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE for System: the failure mode (wrong HMAC branch or accidental queue write) is fully reproducible with an in-process fake HTTP layer; real network adds nothing.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the full request handler (with HMAC middleware) to a spy queue implementation. Send a POST with a body and a deliberately wrong X-Signature-256 header. Assert: response status is 401, and the spy queue records zero write calls.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The ordering constraint — queue write completes before HTTP response is sent — spans two modules: the queue writer and the HTTP responder. A Unit test of either in isolation cannot verify sequencing between them. REAL-INFRA: Sequence can be captured in-process by recording timestamps or using a Promise-tracking stub that captures the relative order of 'queue written' vs 'response sent'. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE for System: the sequencing failure (response sent before queue write) is fully visible in an in-process supertest run with an instrumented queue stub; real network I/O is not required to expose the bug.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler to an instrumented queue stub that records the wall-clock time of each write. Send a valid signed request via supertest and record when the HTTP 200 arrives. Assert that the queue write timestamp precedes the response receipt timestamp.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is about the structural decoupling between enqueue and downstream processing. A Unit test of the handler function could verify it does not await a processing call, but only if the handler is a single composable function — the real failure (accidentally awaiting a downstream Promise) only surfaces when the handler is wired to a real async processing stage. REAL-INFRA: A controllable in-process stub with an unresolved Promise simulates an infinitely slow downstream processor. The test verifies the response resolves while the downstream Promise remains pending — no real network needed. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE for System: the decoupling failure is fully observable by holding a downstream Promise unresolved in-process and checking that supertest receives a response before the Promise resolves.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a downstream processing stub whose Promise never resolves (or resolves after 10 seconds). Send a valid signed request and assert the HTTP response arrives before the downstream Promise resolves, proving the handler does not block on it.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode — event-loop blocking due to slow concurrent handlers — requires multiple simultaneous in-flight requests, which is a multi-module concern (handler concurrency + async scheduling). REAL-INFRA: In Node.js, supertest exercises the real event loop. Sending N concurrent requests with a slow downstream stub (e.g., a 30-second delayed Promise) against an in-process server is sufficient to expose event-loop blocking. A real network adds latency noise but is not required to reveal the bug. MOCK-HIDE: The queue and downstream stubs must be genuinely slow (real setTimeout/delay), not synchronously completing mocks — otherwise the concurrency stress disappears. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE for System: the wall-clock constraint and concurrent load are reproducible in-process; real OS network overhead would obscure the 3-second budget without improving failure detection.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler with a downstream stub that takes 30 seconds per event. Fire 5 concurrent valid requests via supertest. Measure wall-clock elapsed time for each response. Assert all responses arrive within 3 seconds, proving the handler does not serialize on slow downstream work.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Deduplication requires the handler to consult a stateful deduplication store and conditionally skip the queue write — at least two modules interacting. A Unit test of the dedup-check function alone cannot verify that the handler correctly gates the queue write on the dedup result. REAL-INFRA: An in-process dedup store (in-memory or real embedded store) with a spy queue is sufficient. The failure (writing twice despite seeing the same ID) only requires state to persist between two consecutive handler calls — no real network or OS needed. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE for System: the duplicate-write bug is fully detectable in an in-process test by sending the same event ID twice and asserting the queue spy received exactly one write.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a real (or embedded) deduplication store and a spy queue. Send the same signed event (same ID) twice. Assert the queue spy records exactly one write call, and the second request returns a response indicating the duplicate was recognized (e.g., 200 idempotent).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The handler must catch the queue's 'full' signal and translate it into a specific HTTP response shape (503 + Retry-After header). This crosses the boundary between the queue module and the HTTP response layer. REAL-INFRA: A stub queue that throws a 'queue full' error is sufficient — no real queue needed to test the handler's error-handling branch. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE for System: the failure (missing 503, absent Retry-After header) is fully exposed by a supertest call against a handler wired to a queue stub that rejects with a capacity error; real queue infrastructure tests behavior 7, not this one.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler to a queue stub configured to always throw a capacity-exceeded error. Send a valid signed request via supertest. Assert: response status is 503 and the Retry-After header is present with a positive integer value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-HIDE: This behavior explicitly tests the queue implementation itself — not the handler's reaction to a full queue (that is behavior 6). Any stub or mock queue that returns a 'full' error would trivially pass, hiding the real question: does the real queue's capacity enforcement code actually work? REAL-INFRA: 'Persistent queue' implies durable external storage (e.g., Redis/BullMQ, a database-backed queue, or a filesystem journal). Capacity enforcement in such systems involves real write operations against the backing store, atomic counter updates, or storage-layer constraints — none of which are visible to an in-process fake. Testing that the 4th write to a capacity-3 queue is actually rejected requires the real queue process and its backing store to be running. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: an in-process memory-only stub of the queue would never expose a bug in the real queue's capacity logic; you must run the real queue implementation against real storage to observe real enforcement. LLM-DEP: None.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real queue service (and its backing store) in a test environment. Configure capacity = 3. Write 3 events successfully. Attempt a 4th write and assert it is rejected with the expected capacity-exceeded error. Also assert the backing store contains exactly 3 items.
